summary:
the broader picture now.

The broader picture now is the growing tension between the equity market's hope for an earnings-driven melt-up and the bond market’s realization that the geopolitical floor has been removed. We are seeing a collision of regimes.

The evidence lies in the shifting priorities of the three major regions that historically funded the U.S. capital account. For years, the U.S. deficit absorbed excess savings from abroad. Now, those flows are being diverted. The report notes that as these regions prioritize their own infrastructure and military hardware, they are no longer the reliable buyers of U.S. debt they once were.

Stepping back, the cross-cutting signal today is the growing mismatch between equity market optimism and the reality of a structural supply-side shock. This continues the theme we have tracked all week: the collision between an earnings-driven melt-up and a fundamental regime shift in monetary policy.

Stepping back, the core tension today is the divergence between equity factor performance and credit spread resilience. Equities are starting to price in a stagflationary energy shock, with momentum faltering and retail warning of margin pressure. Yet, investment grade credit remains priced for perfection.

the broader picture now.

Your single most important watch item today is the headline flow out of Tehran. A successful mediation by Field Marshal Munir will trigger a massive relief rally in rates and a steepening of the curve, while a breakdown will lock in the stagflationary narrative, forcing a repricing of risk assets that credit markets are currently unprepared for.

Stepping back, the core tension today is the stark divergence between a rates market preparing for a hawkish, inflation-focused Fed and an equity market still priced for an earnings-driven melt-up. This continues the theme we have tracked all week: the collision of regimes.

Stepping back, the core tension today is the widening chasm between a bond market that has declared the free lunch over and an equity market still priced for a soft landing. This continues the theme we have tracked all week: the collision of regimes. On one side, you have the structural supply-side shock of the Iran conflict, hawkish global central banks, and a new, hawkish Fed chair.

Stepping back, the core tension today is the widening chasm between credit and equity markets that are priced for perfection, and a rates market that is flashing structural warning signs. This continues the theme we have tracked all week: the collision of regimes. On one side, we have the structural supply-side shock of the Middle East conflict and a hawkish global central bank consensus.

Stepping back, this continues the theme we have tracked all week: the collision of regimes. We have a stark divergence between a rates market that is pricing in a persistent, energy-driven inflation shock and credit and equity markets that remain priced for absolute perfection. The key takeaway here is that the carry buffer in credit is getting dangerously thin.
Before we close, a look at some research that came across the desk.

Now, depending on where we are in the calendar, this quiet stretch isn't all that unusual. If we are in the dog days of August, the dead of winter around the holidays, or just sitting quietly ahead of a major macro catalyst like a C.P.I. print or a Federal Reserve meeting, the syndicate desks tend to pack up early and issuers sit on their hands.
